ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for stink bean (Parkia speciosa) as a food product; no exact entry for the prepared dish Assam Petai. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Assam Petai as a composite dish. Closest defensible anchor is cooked stink beans. Composite dish aspects (tamarind sauce, sambal) are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Assam Petai or stink bean dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Assam Petai is approximately 100g, representing a side dish portion as commonly served in a Malay or hawker meal.

nutrient_source.title: Assam Petai — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Assam Petai (F09010002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this exact record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — legumes and bean-based dishes analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Assam Petai. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on analogous legume dishes (cooked beans/legumes) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently report GI values of 20–35 for cooked legumes. The very low carbohydrate content (3.3g/100g) results in a glycemic load per 100g serving of approximately 0.8, which is negligible. Estimation basis: Parkia speciosa (petai) is a legume with high dietary fibre (5.32g/100g) and low net carbohydrates, consistent with low-GI legume behaviour.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 245mg/100g from belacan and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 245mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; individuals on a DASH diet should limit portion size to 80–100g and request reduced belacan and dried shrimp when ordering, as these ingredients are the primary sodium contributors in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.3g/100g) and high dietary fibre (5.32g/100g) supporting slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Assam Petai is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.3g/100g), high dietary fibre (5.32g/100g), and estimated low glycemic index (~25), which collectively support stable postprandial blood glucose; however, individuals should verify that the sambal sauce does not contain added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (245mg/100g) offset by high dietary fibre and low saturated fat (~1.2g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Assam Petai provides beneficial dietary fibre (5.32g/100g) and low saturated fat (~1.2g/100g), which are cardioprotective; however, the sodium content of 245mg/100g from shrimp paste and dried shrimp warrants portion control for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, and preparation with reduced belacan is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre and antioxidant-rich ingredients (tamarind, chilli) with low saturated fat; nightshade content warrants monitoring
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Assam Petai is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory fibre content and antioxidant-rich tamarind and chilli; however, individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor joint symptoms, and reduced belacan is recommended to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Assam Petai (亚参臭豆) is a traditional Malay dish featuring petai (stink beans, Parkia speciosa) cooked in a spicy, tangy tamarind-based sambal sauce. The dish is a staple of Malay and Singaporean hawker cuisine, typically prepared by stir-frying petai beans with a sambal paste made from dried chillies, shallots, garlic, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), and dried shrimp, then simmered with tamarind juice (assam) to create a bold, sour-spicy sauce. Petai beans are distinctive for their strong, pungent aroma and slightly bitter, nutty flavour, which is complemented by the umami depth of belacan and the tartness of tamarind. Nutritionally, Assam Petai is relatively low in calories (85 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (3.3g/100g), with a notable protein content (6.3g/100g) and high dietary fibre (5.32g/100g), making it a fibre-rich side dish. Sodium is moderate at 245mg/100g, primarily from belacan and dried shrimp. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index and high fibre; sodium content requires monitoring for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Assam Petai is commonly served as a side dish in Malay rice meals and is available at hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is assam petai suitable for diabetics?
Can hypertension patients eat assam petai?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F09010002) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Malay composite dish preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F09010002 as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at ~1.2g/100g based on typical fat composition of petai-based dishes with small amounts of cooking oil and belacan; this is an approximation as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate based on cooked legume GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Assam Petai was found in approved sources. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this has been retained as a classification note given the very low net carbohydrate content (3.3g/100g), though formal keto certification is not confirmed. The input record listed 'snack' as an occasion tag; this has been noted but the dish is more typically consumed as a side dish in a main meal context.
